Welcome everyone. We've got a great session in store for you today. We have the Dr. Robert Sheldini here. We are going to do a live Q&A today with the Dr. Robert Sheldini as I said. I'm Eileen Cartwright and I will be today's moderator. And as most of you probably know, we usually do not have enough time to get to all of your questions. So today is special. Bob will talk a little bit about our upcoming event, which we are extremely excited about. But then we will open the floor to any questions you have. As always, before we begin, please have your microphones muted unless you're called on. For this session, we will not be using the raised hand feature. Instead, please use the chat area. We will call on you if your question is selected and then you can ask your question directly to Dr. Sheldini. So without further ado, Dr. Sheldini, tell us a little bit about our premier Influence Amplified event here in Phoenix. Well, thank you, Eileen. I share your enthusiasm about this upcoming event on May 10th and 11th here in the Phoenix area. Excuse me, it'll be our first national event like this. So we're very pleased to be able to structure it for something that we're calling Influence Amplified. So it's not just reviewing what we've done before. It has that as a base, of course, but we want to take it to another level. And I'm going to be speaking, I have three presentations, formal ones, as well as an interview, a fireside chat with Dr. Chris Phelps about how I got started being interested in the influence process, how that interest on my part developed, how it was channeled into a scientific and then an applied direction. But as well, I'll be talking about three presentations, in three presentations over the two days. The first is something I'm really learning a lot from because I've been doing a lot of research into the title, which is the systematic study of persuasion, past, present, and future. In other words, has there been an era? It turns out there have been three eras in the 4,000 centuries of recorded human history. There have only been four eras in which persuasion has been systematically studied as a craft. The Periclean age of ancient Greece, the Roman Republic, and the European Renaissance in the past. And the last century has been ours, where with large-scale information programs, campaigns, marketing, advertising, even military kinds of propaganda campaigns and so on, people have been studying the process of influence. So I want to talk about those and how our era is different from any of the previous ones. And that's good because those previous eras ended in an unfortunate way for the influence experts. All right. Then the present, where we are right now, what is the state of the art of systematic studies of influence? And then the future. Where are we going? What will it be like in the next five, 10 years, and maybe even beyond? What are the things that are looming on the horizon that we better get on board with if we're going to optimize our effectiveness? So that's the first presentation I'll make on the first day. Then we'll talk about another presentation, which is the differentiation and the focus on preswasion away from persuasion. What's the difference between those two? Routes to change. And we'll do a deep dive in because we already know a lot about persuasion. And that's what we've been studying for many years. What about preswasion? How does that give us leverage? How does that give us an advantage over those people who simply use persuasion? Because there really is an accelerator component to understanding what you say before you deliver your message designed to persuade. How you set the mindset of the individual who you would like to move in your direction. And this is consistent with something that I'm recognizing as I continue to learn about the influence process. Very often the thing that determines whether a person will say yes to your request is not the feature of your request that is most beneficial to them or most logical or most elegant. Or it's the one that's at the top of consciousness that you have put at top of consciousness before you've made your persuasive appeal. And preswasion does that by allowing us to place prior to our message a concept, an idea, an image that lends itself to the strengths of our message. The things that we have that would make it wisest for people to move in our direction. If we put that at top of consciousness first. Right? That's the channel they're most likely to follow. So we'll talk about preswasion. And then finally here's the one that I'm most excited about because I'm forging a new kind of program. And it has to do with the fact that we need to regain our influence efforts and effectiveness that is being systematically deprived of us through modern communication channels that occur digitally. We are dealing with people so often mediated by technology of one sort or another. And that technology separates us from those individuals in terms of human connection. And if there's anything, if you look back at the history of persuasion science, human connection is the major driver of a scent. So what do we do now when we are dealing with people in separating ways? The way we work is separate from our colleagues. The way we shop is separate. We press a button on Amazon or one of these links. The way we entertain ourselves is separate from one another. We go to Netflix or everything is being done separately. And this is depriving us of the most powerful lever for change. So how do we build human connection back into our communication tools, our digital communication tool, because we're not going to stop the on rushing approach of technology. That's like standing in front of a bullet train. That's foolish. That it's coming down the track. Technology is coming and digital technology is leading the way. That's the front engine. How do we do this? We do it by installing cues and evidence of human connection into our digital technology. So that's the last one of the presentations that I'll make. And then I won't be the only speaker. We've got a list of really high level speakers who will talk about the influence process, the persuasion process as it applies to coaching, as it applies to sales, as it applies to leadership, as it applies to online communication, building organizational cultures, using principles of persuasion. It will be, I think, an interesting and worthwhile event. So that's why we're excited about it. That's why, oh, I just did something I don't like to do. I said, this is why we're so excited about it. Everybody's excited about everything when they're trying to pitch something. We're so excited about this. Everybody uses that term. We're all excited. Here's what I'm going to say. I'm enthusiastic about it. I'm enthusiastic about it. So with that said, and if you have the ability to join us, we'd love to have you there. May 10th and 11th here in the Phoenix area. So you can go on our site, of course, Jaldini.com and get all the information. In the meantime, let's turn to any of your questions or comments that you would like to have me respond to or address today. Yes, Bob, I'm enthusiastic about the event as well because there are things you're going to talk about that I've never even heard you say. And on top of that, I am very enthusiastic about these questions that we have. So let's start out. Karthik, if you want to go ahead and unmute yourself, you can ask your question. Yeah, hi, Eddie. Thank you so much. Hello, Doctor. Just wanted to check with you on this, you know, like, let's say someone is starting off as a professional at work and they are very new to the principles of persuasion. They're just trying to learn about it and trying to understand these principles. So I'm just trying to understand what should be their kind of daily or weekly routines, you know, or patterns of behavior, if I have to put it that way, or rather, routines that would make them, you know, more influential and people start recognizing those patterns immediately. So yeah, that's something I wanted. Excellent question. And then I think has that person gone through our program in your mind? As if they seen the videos and the practitioners program? Is this or somebody who's not even done that? No, that's someone who's I'm trying to coach on now. Yeah. OK, so I think what you can do is give them a little sheet or a card. We have these cards with the seven principles of influence on them and ask them before they ever make a decision, an important decision, not every decision. There are too many of those to check the card and see if there is an opportunity to increase their likelihood of success in a request or a program or a recommendation or a request that they make by enabling the force of one or another of those principles of human behavior, because these are the principles that I'm going to claim are universal. They're the ones that are installed in us from very early points in our lives from socialization, from childhood. But also they have evolved in us as a species. And these are the ones that I think are most likely to be powerful in in moving people because they have always been that way. You know, people have asked me in the past, you know, Dr. Chaldeany, your book, Influence is often called the Bible of online marketing. How could this be? When you wrote that book, there was no online marketing. Right? It didn't exist then. How did you look ahead? How did how could you look forward and see how the these new platforms were going to develop that no one had even thought about at that point? And I always have to say, I didn't look inside. Excuse me, I didn't look ahead. I looked inside us as a species. What are the factors that have always over our history been drivers of a cent to legitimate requests or or recommendations, right? Proposals, right? Those are the ones that will still be in there, right? They don't change over the 40 years since the publication of the book, Influence. They don't change. So those are the ones, if you can ask that person who is new at the process to consider this these this short array of principles, only seven. We can deal with seven. There's actually research that shows people can remember seven plus or minus two ideas, right? They can keep those in memory, right? So we're only up to seven. And if they can refer to those possibilities and you can help them in your coaching with them of steering them to the right one of those possibilities. I think that's your best strategy going forward, graphic. Thank you. We had someone asking about the cards. Yes. Thank you very much. Hi, Eili and hello, Dr. Chaldeany. Thank you very much for the opportunity to ask the question. Look, Chaldeany, I have a potential client who is the training manager of a globally well-known sports car manufacturer. And they are based in Germany. They are very specific. I have tried to sold the program to them and the services and he said, well, you know, I really want to see proof of whether these principles work because what they are trying to do is sell extras. So they sell that the basic model goes really well. Everybody's buying them. But when people come to the shop, then it's very difficult to sell, sell extras like the alloy wheels or the ceramic brakes or whatever they are. And then I gave them the example that we have in the online program, which is the world pools, right? The most expensive world pool being more expensive and how to position it, how to anchor the price and so on. And the thing is that he just sticks to the same thing. I want an example that works in the car industry and works globally. And I don't have an example like that. OK, so here's where we can help you. We do have such an example and it is not only current. It is international. Well, at least it's not in the US. Right. It's the McDonald's example from our colleague in the UK, Stephen Jay Martin, who went to Brazil and Colombia. And made an arrangement with McDonald's franchise owners there who have exactly that problem. How do we sell our add-ons? How do we sell our upgrades? Right. And if you go to a McDonald's anywhere in the world, so your potential client will certainly have been to McDonald's and will know this. People go there and they order a hamburger or a sandwich and a fries and a drink. But McDonald's also sells desserts. Very few people buy their desserts. They have a cookie you can buy. They have a pie, a fruit pie you can buy. But the one that they really want to push is their McFlurry because they have the biggest margin associated with that sale of their McFlurry. And they did the following A, B study. For a period of time, half of the people who came into those McDonald's locations were treated the way they were normally treated by the person behind the counter. After the procedure, after the order was given, the McDonald's employee was trained to say, and would you like a dessert? And the answer was, most of the time, no, by a great amount. No. They're not up. They're not in there for that. The other half were told, would you like a dessert? Our McFlurry is our customer's favorite here. You see how that builds on the two major aspects of social proof, the many and the comparable. Most of our customers who buy desserts choose the McFlurry. It's our most popular dessert for the people here, just like you. Thank you very much. No. Here's the evidence. Your client is right to want the evidence. Purchase of McFlurry rose by 55%. And it was true. The McFlurry was the most popular. He has add-ons. He can just say, this is the one that is our most popular. Or this is the one that is trending in popularity among those of our customers who choose it. Because we've talked about the power of trends. Even if it's not a lot, if it's a trend in that direction, people want it. I know, now let's go back to the most popular. I know if I go into a restaurant, let's say for lunch. And I've had my meal and the waiter comes to the table with the dessert list. He says, would you like a dessert? And I think to myself, I'm going to have a big dinner tonight. Or I've got an event next week and I'd like to lose a couple of pounds before I go. So I might say, no, if he says, or if it's on the dessert list, there's a little asterisk. This is our most popular. Or this is one of our most popular. I get it. Because I suddenly have a reason. I didn't have a reason before to choose that. Not an influence-based reason. Maybe the taste and the description and so on might have gotten. But I didn't have an influence-based reason until they told me which was the most popular. Bob, I think Akoshi is also saying that some of their clients want the example to be in the same industry. And if it isn't, then they don't hold any merit to it. But I think that you've talked about this before in that these are all based on human behavior, basic principles in our human nature. And that they transcend whether you're buying a car or an ice cream or a TV, that these principles still resonate across the board. And that that would need to be brought to the attention. You may not have an exact example, but we're all humans. We all react the same way to these pressures. And here's another piece of evidence in that regard. In Beijing, when a restaurant puts on its menu, this is our most popular item in this category, one of our most popular items. Each one immediately increased in popularity by 13 to 20 percent. So it's not just in South America. It's also in China. This is a part of it, just as you're saying, this is part of a fundamental human tendency to choose based on the popularity. And if you can simply tell people what the popularity of the of the add-on is, or the popularity in terms of a growing popularity of that, you will be significantly more likely to win the day. Now, I'm going to go back one more time to that McDonald's program. And by the way, we can get you the data. We have we have the data in a on a link that we can show you. So you can have it and show your customer. But here's the second part of it. Once you decided on a McFlurry, which now 55 percent of people, more people decide to do 55 percent more. These these McDonald's employees were trained to say, OK, now here are all the add-ons onto the McFlurry. You can get it with chocolate chips. You can get it with cookie dough. You can get it with sprinkles. You can get it right. And they say, and our most popular is the Butterfing finger chunks. And Butterfinger chunks go up 48 percent. This isn't small. These aren't small. And we have the data across international boundaries to make the point that you were making, Eileen, very important point. The one that I was making to Karthik. These are the principles that have always steered people correctly to decide to say yes. There's a reason why these are the principles that work best for us as communicators. If we use them ethically, they work best for the recipients of our communication. They're the ones, if a lot of people are doing it, it must be the right thing. Hey, I should try that. If the authorities are recommending this, this should be, if it's scarce and it's dwindling availability, those, if we inform people of those honest, psychologically related factors, they will do better. Name any of the principles, reciprocity, you will do better as a citizen in your groups if you give back to others who have first given to you. You will be better off. So this is actually something that's similar to what I'll be talking about in that program on the history and persuasion science. And one of the questions I often get about our principles is, well, suppose the person you're dealing with already knows this principle. They already know how commitment and consistency works. They already know how scarce and you use scarcity on them or commitment and consistency. Does that make them more likely to deflect your interest? No, provided that the principle involved will increase their outcomes in the situation, then they want to follow it, even if they know it. And I'll give an example of that during my presentation at our influence, Amplify-Dependent, where it works on me, right? And I'm supposed to be, I'm the one who knows these principles as well as anybody. It still works on me when I can see from the principle that it will lead to good outcomes for me. That trumps everything. That overcomes everything, all doubts and so on. And so as long as we're ethical in the process and it will lead to mutually beneficial outcomes, not only are we entitled to use those principles, that drive people to good choices, it's an honorable thing to do. It's a commendable thing to do because we lead them to better choices. All of these principles, when they are truly in the situation, steer them correctly. Thank you. Thank you for that clarification. Did that help you, Akos? Yeah, thank you. Eileen, let's send everybody on this, that link to the data site where Steve reported the difference between the various kinds of approaches in that. And also he did some other studies as well. But we'll make sure everybody gets that link. Great. Great. Okay, let's go to our next question. I believe it's from Ari. Am I saying that correct? If you're here, you can unmute yourself. Yes, yes. Thank you, Eileen. Okay. Thank you, Dr. Cialini. I hope the event in Arizona will be great. Excellent. I wish I can go there. But you know, I want to mention weakness early on. Dr. Cialini, I'm in Indonesia. It's really far away from Arizona. I already checked the flight. It's really a lot of transit and everything. So we understand it completely. We understand it completely. So I want to pursue it to you. Yeah. Maybe you have a plan to go to Asia maybe or near. Near with Indonesia. So we can meet you. I would love to. Yeah. Maybe you, if you have an event, you will give us information. So this is my question. In the principle of authority, one way to implement amplifier is mention weakness early on. Is it possible then when we acknowledge our weakness or mistakes upfront, it actually creates a less verbal first impression. So it's about first impression. Dr. Cialini, are there any simple tips to ensure that we can successfully build authority when we mention weakness early on? Thank you very much. Yes. Yes. Of course. Another good question. So it turns out that's what we worry about because, but what we are not suggesting is that you sit down in front of a client or a prospect or a customer and say, before I begin, let me tell you all the things that are along with me, my organization, my products and services. No, no, no, no, no, no. But near the beginning, after you have spoken in a generally positive way, but haven't yet got to the specific evidence for the superiority of your products and services, right? You mentioned a weakness, right? But, and that word, but that I just used is important. After you have done that, you have established yourself as a credible source of information who knows not just about the positive features of your, of your products and services and your industry, but you also know about some drawbacks, right? So you're knowledgeable, but secondly, you're trustworthy because you're willing to talk about the drawbacks as well as the positive features, right? But the research shows that when you do that, you increase your credibility in the eyes of the, of the person, right? But how do you get from, oh, this is an honest person to, oh, I want to buy that person's offer. I want to accept that person's offer, right? By using the word, but or however, or nonetheless, and you, you then transfer, you transfer over a bridge, right? By you, by using that word to your strengths. And that will allow them now to see your strengths in a way that they wouldn't have looked at it before, because the information about your strengths is coming from an honest person. So they will process your strengths more deeply and believe them more fully. When you're with a new prospect and you just talk about all of your positive features, the merits of your case, the strongest elements of it and so on. And they don't know yet that you are an honest source of information. There's a wall of doubt between the two of you. They're not sure whether they should believe you about your strengths. Your, and if you send your strongest arguments then, while they are in doubt, they're bouncing off this wall. They don't even get through because they're not sure about the source of that information. But if you mention a weakness that you can defeat in a minute later, you can overwhelm in a minute later, the wall comes down. The doubt about you as a credible presenter of information comes down. And now your strongest argument gets right through. The wall's gone. The wall's not there anymore. So it is a two-step process, not the very first thing you say, but early in your message, you say, you know, we're not as inexpensive as some of our cut rate competitors. But here's what we provide that offers, or, you know, what I'm suggesting may take a little more time to institute. But the savings that will occur after it is implanted, wash out that difference. And now you will be, right, so that's the route. And I would say, two-step process, not at first, mention the strengths and mention, of course, here's something that's true. And then bridge to your strengths. Right. And I think that's the one that works. I know that it works. Have I, maybe I've mentioned this. I'm about to go to the Berkshire Hathaway meetings here in the United States, Warren Buffett's meeting. And I'm a shareholder. Right. And as a result, I get his annual letter to the, to his community, to all the shareholders, the stock owners, stock holders. And here's what I've noticed on the first or second page of every letter at the end of the year. He talks about something that went wrong that year. He mentions a mistake that they made. And then he says, but we've learned from that. That will never happen again. Believe me. That will, he has one. Yeah, so he, he, he does that. And I've also been tracking how often he does that at the beginning of every new section that he comes to. And I've been getting his reports over 25 years now. And he is using this strategy significantly more frequently because he's learned how effective that is. Because now when he says, but, and then provides the evidence of all of the right decisions that were made, you believe him. This is a straight, God, this is a straight shooter talking to me. You believe him now. And it, and for that reason, people hold on to their Berkshire Hathaway stock. I'm one of them. I don't know if I should, maybe I'll tell you a story about how I got my first a share of Berkshire Hathaway stock about 25 years ago. I went to my mailbox and there was a legal sized envelope. I opened it up to find a single share of Berkshire Hathaway stock sent to me by Charlie Munger, Buffett's partner, right, and seemed to be the real brain, the brainiac of the organization, right. And he had read influence. And he said, you know what, your first principle is the principle of reciprocation. You have to give back to those who have benefited you, you have to benefit them. He said, we have made so much money by following the rules of behavioral science that are listed persuasion science. You're entitled to a share of Berkshire Hathaway stock. It was worth $75,000 at that point. Right. I'll tell you, I was in a, by the way, I was in a chair like this one. It's a rolling chair. And I opened this and I, it was like a shock. I went rolling into the wall behind me. Boom. Right. That shot that single share of Berkshire Hathaway stock on this morning's New York Stock Exchange is worth $605,000 a share. There's never been anything like it in the history of our markets. Why? Because Buffett and Munger study human behavior. They read books like influence. Right. They know not only are they in, I'm sorry, Charlie Munger passed away at 99 years and 11 months in December. He missed his birthday by a month. But they, he understood as well as Buffett, how we work. Markets are not made up of econometric models. They're made up of people. And they know how to operate on people to get them to continue to hold their shares, to buy more shares. And they use that in their, in their, their letter to their stockholders every year. So we can do that too. That is a, that is a fantastic story Bob. I love hearing it. It's really touching and it really shows you the power of reciprocity and how these principles are universal. With that said, let's move on. Ezio, would you like to unmute yourself? Hi Dr Chaldini. Good to be with you. Look forward to seeing you in Arizona. I love your perspective on a story. So typically when I'm presenting something and I'm using that example of the saving energy, we know what would influence people to save, save energy. And then, you know, what actually gets people to do it. And if you remember that example, it's, you know, the environmental thing is always seems to come up number one. But in the slide before that, I would have a picture of somebody holding coins in their hand. And when I notice from the audience is everybody does the save money one. Yeah, so I'm connecting the dots of persuasion. It almost happens automatically. That's great. Because it has to do with persuasion. Before they've gotten to the argument you want them to be focused on, right? The value to them of saving energy. You've primed, you've persuaded the concept of economy, right? In mind and they move to it. That's what we're talking about at the beginning. That it's what's at top of consciousness. And if we know what we have, you know, people say about persuasion. Is this some kind of magic? How do you get people to say yes to your request before they know what's in it? You know what's in it. You know the strongest feature of your argument, the merit that is your most powerful aspect and so on. If you can put them in mind of that concept before they get to it, they will zero in on the thing that is strongest for you. It's wisest for them to choose, right? Because you have that. It can be economy. It could be durability. It could be authority, whatever it could be. You put something ahead of that message and you did it with a slide. Brilliant. We all have slide programs. Yeah. I love the story of playing the Italian music, Bob, in the store and that raised the purchase of Italian wine. Right. If you just listening to it. Yes. Yeah. So you go into a wine shop and if they're playing Italian music over the PA system, you're more likely to buy an Italian vintage. If they're playing German music, you're more likely to buy a German vintage. If they're playing French music, you're more likely to buy a French bottle. I mean, so it doesn't even have to be with a word. It has to be just something that brings the concept to top of mind. And it wins the day. And I, you know, some people wonder about the ethical aspect of it. Well, I'm in the store already. I want to buy wine. I'm going to buy some wine, usually. So the fact that you've influenced, presuaded me with music doesn't hurt me at all. Right. I'm still getting what I want. Yes. Right. And I would say if you had a new allotment of reisling, right, or, or Italian, to Mettiva, whatever, whatever it is, and you think that this is really a good choice for people, then play the music that sends them to the best choice they have in your inside your offerings. And that's kind of what we're saying we should all do. Get people to focus on the thing that will steer them correctly to the wisest choice for them to make. Yeah, that's great. Thank you so much. Thank you for that question and an insight as you know that's great. Let's move on to Sylvia, if you're there, would you like to unmute yourself. Dr. Sheldini, I'm very new to the program and to your principles, but I'm currently, you know, developing a program to help job seekers secure a referral. You basically, we mainly use your principles of building the connection to online platforms like, let's say LinkedIn, but any advice you can give us if you can, if you can give me actually to improve the program and get better results for the people that I'm currently working on because the struggle is basically to kind of build the connection to get the people or one on one call that's the main challenge that they're facing. Can you explain to me that helps, but can you explain to me exactly the processes that you use and where where the influence is is necessary is needed. Basically, the process that we use we start by looking for commonalities with the people that we target, we look for something that they have in common, we send them connection requests over to LinkedIn. And you're asking for a referral to a job to a job. So they're looking to get a job in the company that that particular person is working for. I don't know if it's clear. Yeah. And so we start the conversation like we look for commonality we use LinkedIn primarily only LinkedIn. So they look for commonalities with the person they were trying to influence. They send them let's say connection requests and put icing on this commonalities. Then they start conversation and ask the other person about anything about their career about the company that they're currently working any challenges. And once they get some kind of information, they try to provide value. And in terms of value, you know, here, usually can be very small by, let's say commenting on their posts sharing knowledge sharing information that kind of stuff. And after that first interaction, they want to get that person on a call so that this can strengthen their relationship. And during that call, we this is where we ask them for advice, the piece of advice. Usually that advice is very small. It's not something big. They ask them for any course recommendation or book recommendations and something that's going to help them. And then they go and execute onto this advice. They go under the execute and they provide the feedback to that person on the execution of their advice. They want to build credibility. And this is that where they come up with the follow up request, which is about the referral. This is where they mentioned about this particular opening in the company that the person is working for. And this is where they ask for a referral. I don't know. I don't know. It's not very complicated. No, no, those are those are really good strategy, especially like they ask for advice, because you get a partner now in the process. And that's, that's really smart. It and you start out with an email, is that right? No, all the conversation is going through the LinkedIn LinkedIn message. Okay, a LinkedIn message. So, in that message, is there a photograph of the person who is making the request? No, but they can always check his LinkedIn profile where there's a lot of information about his credibility, you know, what he's doing. So there's a credibility is their recommendation. Right. So, I'm, I'm thinking about some new research. It's not brand new, but it's relatively new about saying yes to somebody who you have a picture of next to the request. Right. And you get more yeses. Right. Because there's human connection there. So the study was done in a medical situation. Radiologists who have to look at scans, some, some kind of a CT scan or a bone scan or some kind of scan to find evidence of what might be troubling or problematic features of their, their health. And then they can turn the send their report on to a physician who can follow the treatment properly. So, in this study, half of the, of the scans were sent to radiologists in hospitals. Right. And just the description of this person and this person scan was there. The other half had a picture of the person next to the scan. This is a person. This is a real person. The radiologists spent significantly more time and found significantly more valuable aspects in the scan than if there wasn't a picture next to it. Right. Well, you can see a little bit more about me. Right. So that they have that person's image in mind. You're not just dealing with a case in this instance, you're not just dealing with somebody who is connected with you. It's a person. So the more we can do that, the more we can make the individual making the request personalized, the better we will believe. And, and I do know that a photograph works. And maybe a different photograph in LinkedIn, more personal would help in those situations. Maybe with your family. Yeah. Okay. Yes. Yeah, the reason that people do not like people try to protect their time so they do not want to jump on a call with someone they're very much very protective of their time so how to break into this so that there's more yeses. Okay, more requests. Now you're talking about some research that I've actually done where I can give you a request. So we can get people to say yes to a particular request. If we first make a larger one of on their time and say, if you can't do that, would you do this. So, for example, we went to on a university campus, we asked students, if they would be willing to give a unit of blood at the blood drive on the campus the next day. Right. And so, first we asked them, would you be willing to do that and about 30% of them said yes, I'll sign up for that. And so we said, we're from the, from the area blood services organization and we're looking for people who would be long term blood donors, who, who could be a give a unit of blood every month for the next two years. And people said, no, I don't even know or I'll be in two years, I can't say yes to that. And then we said, Oh, well, would you give a unit of blood tomorrow. Instead of 30% 55% of them say yes. Alright, so, could you say would you be willing to work with me to, to help me with my, my current situation and have a number of calls. If they say yes, great. If they say no, then you get to say well how about just one tomorrow, just for 15 minutes, or you could say, would you be willing to do a 60 minute call tomorrow. Oh no I can't. Well, how about a 15 minute call. And Dr. Shudini then it comes to my, you know, my third question server this, and then what about the third request. It is appropriate. Okay. At that point. But yeah, because you've gotten to know that person better and, and I like asking for you can say you know I really do. And he has a quote that he says, we are always impressed by the wisdom of people who ask for our advice. So, if you ask for their advice. Now it's interesting, we're not impressed by people who ask for advice, generally, they're seen as weak. They're seeing as not knowing what they want.
